title: Week 4 Learning Diary: Policy Applications - The London Plan
text:
Policy G5 (Urban Greening Factor): Requires major developments to contribute to urban greening, quantified through a scoring system that weights different types of green and blue infrastructure. The policy establishes a target Urban Greening Factor (UGF) of 0.4 for predominantly residential developments and 0.3 for commercial.
Policy SI4 (Managing Heat Risk): Requires developments to reduce potential overheating through the cooling hierarchy — minimise internal heat gain, then reduce reliance on air conditioning through passive measures, then use green infrastructure for localised cooling before resorting to mechanical systems.
crumbs: 5  Week 4 Learning Diary: Policy Applications - The London Plan

title: Week 4 Learning Diary: Policy Applications - The London Plan
text:
Application
To demonstrate how EO data directly supports these policy goals, I generated two spatial datasets for Greater London using Landsat 8/9 imagery from summer 2023:

Normalised Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) to map green infrastructure coverage, directly relevant to Policy G5’s greening targets
Land Surface Temperature (LST) to identify heat-vulnerable areas, directly relevant to Policy SI4’s cooling hierarchy










Figure 5.1: NDVI map of Greater London (summer 2023). High values (dark green) indicate dense vegetation — parks, street trees, gardens — providing evapotranspirative cooling. Low values (yellow/brown) indicate impervious surfaces with minimal green cover. The spatial pattern reveals that outer boroughs (Richmond, Bromley, Havering) have substantially higher vegetation fractions than inner boroughs (Tower Hamlets, Southwark, Hackney).











Figure 5.2: Land Surface Temperature (LST) map of Greater London (summer 2023). Red/orange areas indicate high surface temperatures concentrated in the densely built-up urban core, while blue/green areas in outer boroughs and along the Thames corridor show lower temperatures. The spatial correlation with low-NDVI areas is visible.






The spatial relationship between Figure 5.1 and Figure 5.2 illustrates a well-documented pattern: areas with low vegetation cover correspond to elevated land surface temperatures. Li et al. (2022) demonstrated this relationship quantitatively across 11 Texas cities, finding that historically redlined neighbourhoods — which received systematically less green infrastructure investment — exhibit LST values 2–5°C higher than surrounding areas, with corresponding increases in heat-related emergency department visits. Their study used a spatial error model (familiar from CASA0005) to control for spatial autocorrelation, showing that the NDVI-LST-health relationship is not merely a visual correlation but a statistically robust pathway with direct equity implications.
For London specifically, the challenge is translating metropolitan-scale EO evidence into borough-level action. Wellmann et al. (2020) reviewed the use of remote sensing in urban planning and found that while EO data is increasingly cited in academic urban ecology research, its uptake in actual planning instruments remains limited — largely because planners require information at the parcel or block scale, whereas freely available satellite imagery (Landsat at 30 m, Sentinel-2 at 10 m) operates at neighbourhood scale. The authors argue that effective integration requires not just higher resolution data, but intermediary products — heat vulnerability indices, green deficit maps, canopy change alerts — that package raw spectral data into planning-relevant formats.
Martinez and Labib (2023) provide a particularly relevant example, demonstrating how NDVI derived from Sentinel-2 can be used to assess greenness exposure at scales relevant to health policy. They found that the choice of NDVI threshold, buffer distance, and temporal compositing method all significantly affect exposure estimates — meaning that two analyses of the same city using slightly different methodological choices could produce contradictory policy recommendations. This underscores a point made in the lecture: EO data is not self-interpreting, and the analytical decisions between raw imagery and policy-relevant output involve judgement calls that should be made transparent.
The practical implication for the London Plan is that Policies G5 and SI4 could benefit from an operational EO monitoring framework — for example, annual borough-level NDVI composites from Sentinel-2 to track Urban Greening Factor compliance, combined with Landsat-derived LST anomaly maps to identify emerging heat risk hotspots. The Copernicus Urban Atlas already provides land cover classification at 2.5 m resolution for European cities, but it is updated only every 6 years — far too infrequent for monitoring the incremental greening that Policy G5 mandates. A Sentinel-2-based monitoring system operating at annual or seasonal frequency would fill this gap at minimal cost, since the imagery is freely available and the processing can be automated through Google Earth Engine.
crumbs: 5  Week 4 Learning Diary: Policy Applications - The London Plan

title: Week 4 Learning Diary: Policy Applications - The London Plan
text:
Reflection
This week reframed how I think about the relationship between remote sensing and policy. The technical capability to map NDVI and LST across a city is, in a sense, the easy part — what Gerasopoulos et al. (2022) call the “supply side” of Earth observation. The harder challenge is the demand side: understanding what planning officers, public health teams, and elected officials actually need, and packaging EO outputs in formats that connect to their existing workflows and decision timelines. Mapping NDVI and LST for London produces visually compelling outputs, but unless those outputs are linked to specific borough responsibilities, budget cycles, and statutory reporting requirements, they remain academic exercises rather than governance tools.
One tension I found particularly interesting is green gentrification. Policy G5 mandates increased greening in new developments, and the LST evidence clearly shows that the most heat-vulnerable communities are in the densely built-up inner boroughs with the least vegetation. But investing in green infrastructure in these areas — new parks, street trees, green roofs — tends to increase property values and rental costs, potentially displacing the very populations the intervention is meant to protect. This is not a problem that EO data can solve, but it is a problem that EO-informed analysis must acknowledge: spatial targeting of greening interventions without complementary housing affordability protections could worsen the inequalities it claims to address. Li et al. (2022)’s finding that historically disinvested neighbourhoods bear the greatest heat burden makes this especially pointed — the areas most in need of greening are also the areas most vulnerable to displacement if greening succeeds.
Looking ahead, I can see how combining the technical skills from Weeks 1–3 (atmospheric correction, spectral indices, texture analysis) with the policy framing from this week could produce genuinely useful analysis. For example, a time-series analysis of Sentinel-2 NDVI across London boroughs could quantify whether Policy G5 is actually delivering measurable increases in vegetation cover since its adoption — a question that the GLA currently has no systematic way to answer at scale.

title: Week 7: Advanced Classification
text:
Reflection
The headline finding from this week — 99.6% overall accuracy — is precisely the kind of result that should trigger scepticism rather than celebration. As Karasiak et al. (2022) demonstrate, spatial dependence between training and test sets is the most common and most consequential pitfall in remote sensing accuracy assessment. In my practical, validation points were extracted from the same buffered polygons used for training. Because neighbouring pixels share atmospheric conditions, illumination geometry, and land cover characteristics (Tobler’s First Law), the testing data was statistically correlated with the training data — meaning the accuracy estimate reflects the model’s ability to interpolate within known regions rather than generalise to unseen areas.
A more rigorous approach would implement spatial cross-validation — for example, dividing the study area into spatially disjoint blocks and ensuring that no block contributes pixels to both training and testing sets. Karasiak et al. (2022) showed that this typically reduces reported accuracy by 10–15 percentage points, which would bring my result closer to a realistic 85–90% — still respectable, but a fundamentally different claim than 99.6%. This distinction matters for policy applications: if the GLA were to use an OBIA classification to monitor Policy G5 (Urban Greening Factor) compliance, a model that actually achieves 85% accuracy would produce measurably different greening estimates than one assumed to achieve 99%.
The Kappa coefficient debate is also instructive. Kappa attempts to quantify agreement beyond chance by comparing observed accuracy to the accuracy expected if the classifier assigned classes randomly in proportion to their marginal frequencies. However, as the lecture noted, this “expected agreement” baseline is arbitrary — there is no reason to assume that a random proportional classifier is the correct null model. In practice, Kappa values are almost always lower than OA but highly correlated with it, adding no independent information. The emerging consensus in the literature is to report the full confusion matrix with PA and UA per class, supplemented by the F1 score for imbalanced datasets, rather than relying on any single summary metric.
Looking forward, the progression across Weeks 6 and 7 — from pixel-based RF to OBIA with SNIC — has demonstrated that classification is not primarily a machine learning problem but a feature engineering and validation design problem. The RF algorithm was identical in both weeks; what changed was the input representation (pixels vs. objects with derived features) and, implicitly, the spatial logic embedded in the workflow. For my own future work, the key takeaway is that time spent on thoughtful segmentation, feature construction, and rigorous validation design will yield larger accuracy improvements than experimenting with more complex classifiers.





Foody, Giles M. 2004. “Sub-Pixel Methods in Remote Sensing.” In Remote Sensing Image Analysis: Including the Spatial Domain, edited by Steven M. de Jong and Freek D. van der Meer, 37–49. Dordrecht: Springer.


Karasiak, Nicolas, Jean-François Dejoux, Claude Monteil, and David Sheeren. 2022. “Spatial Dependence Between Training and Test Sets: Another Pitfall of Classification Accuracy Assessment in Remote Sensing.” Machine Learning 111: 2715–40.

title: Week 8: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR)
text:
Summary
This week introduced Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) — an active sensor that fundamentally differs from the passive optical systems (Landsat, Sentinel-2) we have worked with throughout the module. Where optical sensors passively record reflected sunlight, SAR transmits its own microwave pulses and records the returned signal (backscatter), enabling all-weather, day-and-night imaging — a capability the lecture illustrated vividly with Sentinel-1 flood mapping during the 2022 Australian La Niña event, where SAR provided imagery while persistent cloud cover rendered optical sensors useless.
The “synthetic aperture” concept is the core engineering insight. A longer physical antenna produces a narrower beam and higher resolution, but we cannot mount a kilometre-long antenna on a satellite. Instead, SAR synthesises a long antenna by combining echoes received as the satellite moves along its flight track — each ground pixel is imaged many times from slightly different positions, and the phase differences between these returns are used to reconstruct a high-resolution image. This is analogous to echolocation in bats, as the lecture noted — emitting a signal and interpreting what bounces back.
A SAR signal carries two types of information: amplitude (backscatter) and phase. Backscatter intensity depends on three surface properties: roughness (relative to the wavelength — smooth surfaces like calm water produce specular reflection away from the sensor, while rough surfaces scatter energy back), permittivity/dielectric constant (related to moisture content — wetter surfaces are more reflective to radar), and geometric structure (the double-bounce effect between vertical walls and horizontal ground produces exceptionally strong returns from urban areas). Phase records the position of the wave on its cycle when it returns, which is critical for interferometric applications (InSAR) — comparing phase differences between two acquisitions to detect surface deformation at millimetre precision. DInSAR extends this by removing the topographic contribution using a DEM, isolating the displacement signal.
Polarisation adds further discriminatory power. Sentinel-1 operates in dual polarisation: VV (vertical transmit, vertical receive) is most sensitive to surface roughness (e.g., bare earth, water state), while VH (vertical transmit, horizontal receive) responds to volume scattering within complex three-dimensional structures like vegetation canopies, where the polarisation of the wave is rotated by multiple internal reflections (Dabboor and Brisco 2018). Double-bounce scattering from buildings is most sensitive to HH polarisation, though Sentinel-1 does not acquire HH in its standard IW mode — a limitation for urban applications.
The lecture also clarified the three data scales — an important practical distinction. SAR data can be stored as power (raw values, good for statistical analysis but poor for visualisation since bright areas dominate), amplitude (square root of power, better visual balance), or dB (10 × log₁₀ of power, good for identifying differences in dark pixels — the default in GEE). A paper that says it “used SAR data” without specifying which scale was used has left a critical methodological detail unstated. In GEE, Sentinel-1 GRD data is provided in dB scale by default; to perform calculations (e.g., ratios, means), we must convert back to power using ee.ImageCollection('COPERNICUS/S1_GRD_FLOAT') or by applying 10^(dB/10).

title: Week 8: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR)
text:
Application
The second half of the lecture focused on change detection with SAR, where the approaches differ fundamentally from optical change detection due to SAR’s statistical properties.
Simple image subtraction — the default approach for optical change detection — is inappropriate for SAR because SAR backscatter follows a multiplicative noise model (speckle is proportional to signal intensity), meaning difference pixels in bright areas will have inherently higher variance than in dark areas, regardless of whether change has occurred. The lecture presented a progression of alternatives. Ratio images (\(ratio = \frac{image_2}{image_1}\)) are more appropriate because division normalises the multiplicative noise. The improved ratio (\(IR = 1 - \frac{min(I_1, I_2)}{max(I_1, I_2)}\)) amplifies the change signal by ensuring changed pixels deviate further from zero. Log ratio (\(ln\frac{image_2}{image_1}\)) converts the multiplicative relationship to an additive one, and Zhuang et al. (2019) tested these approaches against reference datasets for three study areas (Berne, Ottawa, FengFeng), finding — contrary to common assumption — that the standard ratio outperformed the log ratio in several scenarios, challenging the field’s received wisdom.
However, all two-image approaches discard the temporal depth of the full image collection. The lecture proposed a more powerful framework: computing the standard deviation over time for each pixel across the entire collection. If a pixel has undergone change (e.g., a building constructed, a forest cleared, an area flooded), its backscatter values across the collection will exhibit much higher temporal variance than a stable pixel. Thresholding this variance image — and evaluating the threshold using an ROC curve against validation data — provides a principled approach to change detection that leverages SAR’s high temporal frequency (6–12 day revisit) rather than reducing it to two snapshots.
Data fusion between SAR and optical imagery represents the most comprehensive approach. Schulte to Bühne and Pettorelli (2018) identify three fusion levels. Decision-level fusion simply appends SAR bands to the optical band stack and classifies the combined dataset — the approach most directly compatible with the RF and OBIA workflows from Weeks 6–7. Object-level fusion first segments the imagery into objects (via SNIC or similar), then combines optical and SAR features per object before classification. Image-level fusion creates entirely new pixel values by integrating both data sources — for example, through Intensity-Hue-Saturation (IHS) transformation, where the intensity channel of an optical image is replaced with SAR backscatter before converting back to RGB, producing a composite that carries SAR’s structural information within an optically intuitive colour space. The lecture noted that GEE provides rgbToHsv and hsvToRgb functions that can be adapted for this purpose, though true IHS transformation requires custom implementation.
For urban monitoring in London (connecting back to the Week 4 policy analysis), SAR-optical fusion has a specific practical advantage: London’s persistent cloud cover limits the availability of cloud-free optical composites, particularly in winter. A fused Sentinel-1/Sentinel-2 workflow could provide year-round land cover monitoring independent of atmospheric conditions — directly addressing the temporal gap that would undermine an optical-only monitoring system for Policy G5 (Urban Greening Factor) compliance.
crumbs: 9  Week 8: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR)

title: Week 8: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR)
text:
Reflection
The most significant conceptual shift this week was understanding that SAR and optical data are not interchangeable alternatives but fundamentally different physical representations of the same landscape. Optical sensors measure the interaction between sunlight and surface chemistry (molecular absorption, pigment concentration); SAR measures the interaction between transmitted microwaves and surface geometry (roughness, structure, moisture). A park in an optical image appears as a high-NDVI green patch defined by chlorophyll; the same park in a SAR image appears as a moderate-backscatter texture defined by volume scattering within the canopy. Neither is more “correct” — they encode different physical properties of the same objects.
This has direct implications for problems encountered earlier in the module. In Week 6, my pixel-based classification of London suffered from spectral confusion between building shadows and water — both have low optical reflectance. SAR would not face this confusion at all: water produces very low backscatter (specular reflection), while shadow has no effect on SAR because SAR provides its own illumination — there are no shadows in a SAR image (though there are radar shadows from geometric effects, which are a different phenomenon). A fused classification incorporating Sentinel-2 spectral bands and Sentinel-1 VV/VH backscatter would likely resolve this confusion at the physics level rather than through the NDWI workaround I employed — a more principled solution that leverages the complementary sensing modalities.
The change detection discussion raised an interesting methodological question that I haven’t fully resolved. The lecture showed that there is no consensus on the best SAR change detection approach — ratio vs. log ratio vs. improved ratio vs. temporal variance — and that the “best” method depends on the specific dataset and application context. Zhuang et al. (2019)’s finding that simple ratios can outperform log ratios contradicts a widely held assumption in the field. This suggests that the SAR change detection literature may contain methodological conventions that are not as well-founded as they appear, and that empirical comparison against reference data (via ROC curves) is essential rather than defaulting to whichever approach a previous paper used. For my own future work, the takeaway is that SAR analysis requires more careful justification of methodological choices than optical analysis, where the workflows (atmospheric correction → spectral indices → classification) are more standardised.
Finally, a practical tension: SAR imagery is visually unintuitive. A true-colour optical composite looks like a photograph; a SAR image is a grainy, speckled greyscale where cities are bright and water is black. This interpretive barrier may partly explain why SAR remains underutilised in policy contexts despite its operational advantages — planning officers who can immediately understand an NDVI map may struggle to extract meaning from a VH backscatter image. This reinforces the argument from Week 4 that derived products (urban footprint maps, flood extent polygons, change detection alerts) are more likely to achieve policy uptake than raw sensor imagery, regardless of how technically superior the underlying data source is.




Dabboor, Mohammed, and Brian Brisco. 2018. “Wetland Monitoring and Mapping Using Synthetic Aperture Radar.” In Wetlands Management: Assessing Risk and Sustainable Solutions. IntechOpen.


Schulte to Bühne, Henrike, and Nathalie Pettorelli. 2018. “Better Together: Integrating and Fusing Multispectral and Radar Satellite Imagery to Inform Biodiversity Monitoring, Ecological Research and Conservation Science.” Methods in Ecology and Evolution 9 (4): 849–65.


Zhuang, Hao, Kazhong Deng, Hongdong Fan, and Yonghui Yu. 2019. “It Is a Misunderstanding That Log Ratio Outperforms Ratio in Change Detection of SAR Images.” European Journal of Remote Sensing 52 (1): 515–23.
